input.new_string:
3. **`verify` opt-in label** — a single flat Linear label that opts a ticket into the new flow. Default (no label) is unchanged; ops/KTLO and simple bugfix tickets continue to drain as today. Generalised to a `flow:` label group only if/when a second flow is introduced — premature for v1 (per review c_dacf16). Parser added to `labels.py` alongside the existing `repo:` / `model:` resolution; no group machinery yet.
4. **Run-log schema + OTel attribute extension** *(prerequisite for KR1; absorbs former KR3 instrumentation)* — add two new fields per run-log entry: `flow` (string, e.g. `"verify"`, or `null` for default flow) and `verifier_verdict` (object: `{result, findings[], invoked_at}` or `null` if the flow didn't run a verifier). Add matching attributes on the `drain.issue` OTel span. Schema is additive — existing run logs and `grade` continue to work unchanged.
5–6. **Worker integration: planner pre-step** — when `verify` label is set, invoke `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` as the first action in the worker session; capture the AC checklist into the run log; halt if the planner can't produce one (covered by slice 9's halt machinery).
7–8. **Worker integration: verifier post-step** — when `verify` label is set, invoke `/code-review-and-quality` against working-tree changes before Done transition; record the verdict in `verifier_verdict`; gate the Linear Done transition on a pass.
9. **Halt-on-verifier-fail + revert path** — extends decision §8 / §9 machinery: on verifier fail, leave worktree, halt cycle, name the breach in `cycle_halt_reason`.
10. **Grading workflow** — (a) per-ticket grade template at `~/.drain-cycle/grades/<issue>.md` matching KR1's questions; (b) `drain-cycle grade --flow=verify` subcommand reading the templates and reporting pass-count / pass-rate across a ticket window; (c) silent-Done detection (KR2 grader): fail any run-log entry where `flow == "verify"` AND `final_linear_state == "completed"` AND `verifier_verdict` is absent.
11. **Cost-impact handling** — measure per-ticket token multiplier under the new flow; add `verify`-flow-specific overrides in `limits.yml` if cycle cap pressure shows up.
12. **`docs/design-decisions.md` §1 supersession** — write a successor decision explaining the verifier-gated Done contract; mark §1 as superseded with a link to the new entry.

content.text: Now §8 (kill condition — remove orphaned KR3 reference) and §10 (open review points for round 2):

input.old_string:
## §8 Kill condition

If, after running contract-enabled flows on **5+ complex tickets across 2 cycles**, the verifier produces **zero true-positive findings** (every halt was a false positive, and verifier-passed Dones don't visibly improve PR-review outcomes vs non-contract drains) — the planner+verifier overhead isn't earning its keep.

Action on kill: revert the contract enforcement (the `flow:` label becomes a no-op or is removed); keep the instrumentation (skill-sequence trace stays in run log + OTel); record the negative result as a new decision in `docs/design-decisions.md`.

**Open question:** is "operator-touch events per cycle > 2" (the §6.3.B target) also a kill trigger, or only a "tune the contract" trigger? If yes, append to this condition.
input.new_string:
## §8 Kill condition

If, after running the new flow on **5+ hard tickets across 2 cycles**, the verifier produces **zero true-positive findings** (every verifier halt was a false positive, and verifier-passed Dones don't visibly improve PR-review outcomes vs default-flow drains) — the planner+verifier overhead isn't earning its keep.

Action on kill: revert the contract enforcement (the `verify` label becomes a no-op or is removed); keep the recording fields (`flow` and `verifier_verdict` stay in run log + OTel — they're cheap and useful for future investigation); record the negative result as a new decision in `docs/design-decisions.md`.

-1. **§6.3 — KR3 slot:** A (foundation), B (maintenance, blocked by rule 5b), or C (both, with 4 KRs, also blocked unless one dimension reclassified). Recommendation: **A**.
-2. **§6.1 — KR1 grading:** is "no operator-required fix" verified manually, or via a stricter automated proxy (e.g. zero post-drain operator commits)?
-3. **§6.2 — KR2 measurement window:** "every contract-enabled cycle for the initiative's duration" — fine, or should it cap (e.g. first 10 cycles) for grading discipline?
-4. **§7 — appetite shape:** ~12 issues feels right; any slice you'd drop or split?
-5. **§8 — kill condition:** should operator-touch volume be a kill trigger or only a tuning trigger?
-6. **§9 — out-of-scope:** anything in here that should actually be in scope, or anything in-scope above that you'd push out?
+**Resolved in round 1 (round 1 comment IDs in brackets):**
+- ~~§6 KRs need plain English~~ [c_7918fa] — rewritten with `plain-English version:` lines and audit-footer-only rubric vocabulary.

+- ~~§6.1 — clarify what's new (metrics/logs?)~~ [c_b1b472] — KR1's how-we'll-grade-it now spells out the four concrete artefacts (two run-log fields, two OTel attributes, the grade markdown, the `--flow` subcommand). No new streaming logs/metrics.
+- ~~§6.1 — single-trial too weak; need template for multi-ticket grading~~ [c_d29df7] — moved to ≥ 3 of 5; per-ticket grade template added as slice 10(a).

+- ~~§6.3 — KR3 redundant with KR1; offered override~~ [c_a2a5ee] — KR3 dropped; instrumentation work folded into KR1's how-we'll-know and explicitly scoped as slice 4. Rule 4 override documented inline.
+- ~~§7 slice 3 — `flow:` label premature abstraction~~ [c_dacf16] — replaced with a single flat `verify` opt-in label; group machinery deferred until a second flow appears.
+**Open for round 2:**
+1. **§6.1 KR1 rate (≥ 3 of 5)** — 60 % first-pass merge-clean rate. Right ambition for the bet, or too high / too low?

+2. **§6.1 KR1 grade-template authorship** — should the per-ticket grade markdown be filled in by you manually, by a separate grading agent invoked after each drain, or both (agent fills draft, you confirm)?
+3. **§6.2 KR2 cap** — "cap at 10 cycles if the initiative runs long" is the new wording. Fine, or a different cap?
+4. **§7 slice 4 naming** — slice 4 is now KR1's prerequisite (formerly KR3 work). Naming reads well, but flag if you'd reframe.
+5. **§7 appetite** — ~12 issues unchanged. Any slice you'd still drop or split now that the contents are clearer?

+6. **§8 kill condition** — round-1 question about operator-touch volume as kill trigger was implicit in dropped KR3; not re-asked. Confirm you're fine with kill being purely "verifier produced no true positives," with operator-touch volume living only as a soft tuning signal not codified in the kill condition.
+7. **§9 out-of-scope** — anything that should actually be in scope, or vice versa?
## §11 Next steps
1. You leave inline feedback on this doc.

Run `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` interactively on 3 real tickets from a recent cycle (one well-formed, one ambiguous, one you'd call "unsliceable"). Output: a written recommendation in `docs/planner-spike-findings.md` answering — did the skill produce useful AC checklists on all three? did it surface ambiguities the ticket missed? did the "unsliceable" one actually yield a checklist (i.e. was it slicing-resistant, or just under-decomposed)? Decision at the end: proceed / reshape / kill before slice 2. No code changes in this slice; if the spike finds `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` isn't the right skill for the planner role, slice 2's design doc names an alternative (existing skill, new skill, or bespoke prompt).

lines: Indicative slice sketch (planning-and-task-breakdown produces the real list later; not a commitment to this exact decomposition):

-1. **Planner spike** — does a fresh-context planner produce useful AC checklists on 3 real tickets, including one "unsliceable" one? Output: written recommendation; decision to proceed/reshape/kill before slice 2.

+1. **Planner spike** — operator-driven manual investigation, not a drained ticket. Run `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` interactively on 3 real tickets from a recent cycle (one well-formed, one ambiguous, one you'd call "unsliceable"). Output: a written recommendation in `docs/planner-spike-findings.md` answering — did the skill produce useful AC checklists on all three? did it surface ambiguities the ticket missed? did the "unsliceable" one actually yield a checklist (i.e. was it slicing-resistant, or just under-decomposed)? Decision at the end: proceed / reshape / kill before slice 2. No code changes in this slice; if the spike finds `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` isn't the right skill for the planner role, slice 2's design doc names an alternative (existing skill, new skill, or bespoke prompt).

2. **shape:design-doc** — architecture for the required-skill-sequence contract: label mechanism, prompt structure, halt behaviour, run-log schema, OTel attribute shape, decision §1 supersession path.

3. **`verify` opt-in label** — a single flat Linear label that opts a ticket into the new flow. Default (no label) is unchanged; ops/KTLO and simple bugfix tickets continue to drain as today. Generalised to a `flow:` label group only if/when a second flow is introduced — premature for v1 (per review c_dacf16). Parser added to `labels.py` alongside the existing `repo:` / `model:` resolution; no group machinery yet.

4. **Run-log schema + OTel attribute extension** *(prerequisite for KR1; absorbs former KR3 instrumentation)* — add two new fields per run-log entry: `flow` (string, e.g. `"verify"`, or `null` for default flow) and `verifier_verdict` (object: `{result, findings[], invoked_at}` or `null` if the flow didn't run a verifier). Add matching attributes on the `drain.issue` OTel span. Schema is additive — existing run logs and `grade` continue to work unchanged.

input.old_string: 10. **Grading workflow** — (a) per-ticket grade template at `~/.drain-cycle/grades/<issue>.md` matching KR1's questions; (b) `drain-cycle grade --flow=verify` subcommand reading the templates and reporting pass-count / pass-rate across a ticket window; (c) silent-Done detection (KR2 grader): fail any run-log entry where `flow == "verify"` AND `final_linear_state == "completed"` AND `verifier_verdict` is absent.
input.new_string:
10. **Grading workflow** — designed for low-friction operator confirmation rather than blank-page authorship (per round-2 review c_1d55a4):
    1. **Per-ticket grade markdown template** at `~/.drain-cycle/grades/<issue>.md` — checklist matching KR1's questions plus a `status:` field (`draft` / `confirmed`).
    2. **Auto-draft on completion** — when a contract-enabled drain finishes, drain-cycle invokes a one-shot grading agent (single `claude -p` call) with the ticket body + run-log entry + diff, and writes a `status: draft` file at the template path with the agent's first-pass answers. Operator opens the file, reviews, edits where the agent got it wrong, flips `status: confirmed`. Less friction than authoring blank; still operator-owned at the moment of judgement.
    3. **`drain-cycle grade --flow=verify` subcommand** — reads grade files with `status: confirmed`, reports pass-count and pass-rate across a ticket window; warns about un-confirmed drafts so they don't get forgotten.
    4. **Silent-Done detection** (KR2 grader): fail any run-log entry where `flow == "verify"` AND `final_linear_state == "completed"` AND `verifier_verdict` is absent.

input.command:
crit comment --json --author 'Claude Code' <<'CRITJSON'
[
  {"reply_to": "c_1d55a4", "body": "Friction caught — restructured slice 10 around auto-draft + operator-confirm rather than blank-page authorship. New sub-steps:

1. **Template** — `~/.drain-cycle/grades/<issue>.md` with checklist + `status:` field (`draft` / `confirmed`).
2. **Auto-draft on drain completion** — drain-cycle invokes a one-shot grading agent (single `claude -p`) with ticket body + run-log entry + diff, writes the file with `status: draft` and the agent's first-pass answers. You open the file, review, edit where the agent got it wrong, flip to `confirmed`.
3. **`drain-cycle grade --flow=verify`** — reads only `confirmed` files, reports pass-rate, warns about un-confirmed drafts so they don't get lost.
4. **Silent-Done detection** (KR2 grader).

The judgement step stays operator-owned (you decide pass/fail per ticket), but the data-entry step is automated. If this still feels like too much friction we can iterate further in round 3."},
  {"reply_to": "c_befa07", "body": "Clarified slice 1. The spike is **operator-driven manual investigation, not a drained ticket** — you invoke `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` interactively on 3 real tickets from a recent cycle (one well-formed, one ambiguous, one you'd call "unsliceable"). Output is `docs/planner-spike-findings.md` answering whether the skill produced useful AC checklists, whether the "unsliceable" ticket actually yielded one, and a proceed / reshape / kill decision before slice 2 (the design doc).

Why not a drained ticket: the spike is meta-work *about* what the worker should do, and we don't have the worker-side flow built yet to drain it through. It's the small upfront investment that the design doc (slice 2) then builds on. If `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` turns out to be the wrong skill for the role, the design doc names an alternative (existing skill, new skill, or bespoke prompt embedded in the worker's prompt)."}
]
CRITJSON

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The conversation progressed through five intents:
   
   a) **Initial comparison**: User asked how `gastown` (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown) compares to drain-cycle, with the hard constraint that any alternative must work with their Claude Code/Codex subscription, not an API key license model.
   
   b) **Inter-agent verification exploration**: User identified the gap that complex Linear tickets that can't be sliced further get drained today but the agent forgets details or under-verifies. Wanted to know if gastown has agents that check each other.
   
   c) **Multi-surface collaboration model**: User stated collaboration applies to BOTH inputs (the ticket itself) and outputs (the code), and includes human-in-the-loop. Plan reviews via crit work; code reviews via Graphite stacked PRs have no automatic feedback loop.
   
   d) **Unifying philosophy**: User stated "manual skills + any MCPs i build, should be invokable by this agent — in essence the philosophy is that there are lots of small things that can be changed together (orchestrated). you can either do them manually or you feed tickets into Linear and the agent will do it for you."
   
   e) **Formal initiative shaping**: User invoked `/shape:initiative` to shape this multi-agent collaboration direction into a proper Linear initiative, then used `/crit:crit` for iterative review of the draft.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - drain-cycle: Python single-user CLI orchestrating `claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions` sessions per Linear issue in isolated git worktrees
   - gastown: Go-based multi-agent workspace coordinator (Mayor/Polecats/Hooks/Convoys/Witness/Deacon/Refinery) with Beads as task source
   - Type 2 (personal product, single-user) initiative classification — KR mix: correctness + no-silent-failure + maintenance-burden
   - OKR-shaped initiatives: Goal sentence (Objective) + 3 KRs with baseline/target/measured over/how-we'll-know
   - KR role tags: bet (the push) / brake (don't regress) / foundation (instrumentation enabling measurement)
   - KR commitment tags: (commit) — must hit 1.0 / (stretch) — aspirational, 0.6-0.7 = success
   - Layer 1 dimensions: correctness / outcome / maintenance / discipline
   - Layer 2 templates: golden-path test / self-trial protocol / structural cap + revisit gate / artefact-exists + sections-complete
   - 11-rule verification rubric at Step 6.5 of initiative-shape
   - Evaluator-optimizer (actor-critic) loop pattern
   - Design decision §1 supersession: agent self-asserts Done → verifier-gated Done
   - OTel tracing to Honeycomb (already shipped in commit 3eae2f5)
   - Subscription auth only (no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / OPENAI_API_KEY)
   - crit-cli for inline review with auto-reload and live daemon
   - Skills + MCPs as composable primitives (UNIX-philosophy for agent work)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `/Users/anton/.claude/projects/-Users-anton-src-drain-cycle/memory/project-direction-agent-collaboration.md` (created)
     - Captures the strategic direction shift toward multi-agent collaboration for correctness
     - Three surfaces: input refinement, output verification, PR feedback loop
     - Updated mid-conversation with the unifying philosophy (skills as composable primitives)
   
   - `/Users/anton/.claude/projects/-Users-anton-src-drain-cycle/memory/MEMORY.md` (appended)
     - Added pointer line for new memory file
   
   - `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md` (created and iteratively revised through 2 rounds of crit)
     - Current §6 KRs (after round 2):
       - **§6.0 Goal**: "For the operator running drain-cycle on complex tickets that can't be sliced further, we want a second agent to verify the produced code before the ticket is marked Done — so that incomplete or under-verified work doesn't silently land on main."
       - **§6.1 KR1 (stretch) — Of the next 5 hard tickets drained with the new flow, ≥ 3 ship and merge with no operator-pushed fix commits *(bet)***
       - **§6.2 KR2 (commit) — Every ticket reaching Done in Linear under the new flow has a recorded second-agent review verdict in the run log; zero exceptions *(brake)***
       - **§6.3 KR count — manual override taken** (KR3 dropped; rule 4 will fail at Step 6.5, override documented)
     - §7 Appetite: ~12 issues, indicative slice sketch with slice 1 (planner spike), slice 4 (run-log + OTel schema extension), slice 10 (grading workflow with auto-draft)

     - §10 Round 3 open review points (7 → 6 open after round 2)
   
   - Key code patterns referenced (but not modified):

5. Problem Solving:

   - Identified that the gap user wants filled (inter-task multi-agent review) does NOT exist in gastown

   - Currently waiting on round 3 crit feedback

6. All user messages:

   - "/crit:crit docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md" (invoked crit skill)

     - c_befa07: "is there a particular skill that should be used for this? or is this the first ticket in the queue that will be added with all the right context so that it can be executed?"
   
   **Security/constraint instructions stated in this session and global CLAUDE.md** (preserve verbatim):

   - "Do not add Co-Authored-By trailers" (drain-cycle's AGENTS.md, overrides global Co-Authored-By instruction)
   - "Do not write, in any comment or docstring: Linear issue / ticket IDs (ABA-NNN)... Commit SHAs or PR numbers... Fix-history narration"
   - "An issue isn't Done if work only exists locally" — completion requires Review → Fix → Commit+push → Summary comment → Done state transition
   - "This repo pushes directly to main; PRs only when the owner asks"
   - drain-cycle workers run with `--dangerously-skip-permissions` — operator responsibility for scope discipline at cycle planning

7. Pending Tasks:

   - Slice 1 (planner spike) clarified as operator-driven manual investigation using `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown`, output going to `docs/planner-spike-findings.md`

When the task notification arrives indicating crit round 3 is complete, read the review file at `/Users/anton/.crit/reviews/b8546ee2cec9/review.json`, identify any new unresolved comments, address them with file edits, post replies via `crit comment --json --author 'Claude Code'`, and launch round 4 via `crit docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md`.

file.content:
---
name: project-direction-agent-collaboration
description: "drain-cycle's strategic direction — multi-agent collaboration for correctness across three surfaces (input refinement, output verification, PR-feedback loop), not throughput"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: e3453eb4-1dd7-4bd0-b6b9-8d80b84b89f1
---

drain-cycle is evolving from "drain a Linear cycle unattended" toward "a batch/unattended driver for an ecosystem of small composable primitives (skills + MCPs), with Linear tickets as the work queue." Evolved piece by piece, no single design committed yet.

## Unifying philosophy

UNIX-philosophy for agent work:
- **Skills + MCPs** = the small composable tools (every skill the operator has manually, the worker has too — decision 10's `.claude/` symlink already arranges this)
- **Linear ticket** = the work-queue entry (the operator's intent as data)
- **Worker session** = the shell that runs the composition
- **drain-cycle** = cron/dispatch for the queue
- **Manual Claude Code** = interactive equivalent

Two interfaces to the same primitives: do it yourself manually, or feed a ticket to Linear and the agent does it for you. The agent has the same toolkit you do.

Implication: most "build a custom multi-agent pipeline" framings collapse. The verifier is `/code-review-and-quality` invoked late in the worker session, not a custom subprocess. The planner is `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown` invoked early. The pieces exist as skills; what's missing is the **contract** for which skills must run, in what order, before Done.

Anchored principles:
- **correctness > throughput** — operator's stated value; throughput is "easy to solve" and not the bet
- **collaboration on inputs AND outputs**, not just outputs — even well-formed tickets may need refinement before implementation
- **human-in-the-loop is part of the system**, not orthogonal — plan reviews via `crit`, code reviews via Graphite stacked PRs (see [[drain-output-to-stacked-prs]])
- **subscription auth only** — Claude Code / Codex CLI subprocess, no API-key models; rules out GitHub Actions / claude-code-action for the PR loop
- gastown evaluated and rejected — its bet is the opposite (throughput, slice into beads, parallel fan-out, weak correctness)

Three collaboration surfaces identified:
1. **Input refinement** — pre-implementation ticket critique (does it pass implementation-ready bar? what's ambiguous? what AC is missing?). The "unsliceable ticket" claim is itself a hypothesis worth testing: a fresh-context planner that can produce an AC checklist is implicitly slicing. Maps to existing skills (`/shape:plan-review`, `/shape:planning-and-task-breakdown`).
2. **Output verification** — post-implementation but pre-merge. Supersedes design decision 1 (agent self-asserts Done): a fresh-context verifier is the better proxy decision 1 already gestured at. Maps to existing `/code-review-and-quality` (already mandated by linear-workflow.md but not currently enforced by the worker).
3. **PR feedback loop** — autonomous response to human review comments on Graphite PRs. The only currently *unautomated* surface; requires a local trigger model (polling daemon / one-keystroke / hook) because subscription-auth rules out GitHub-side automation. Under the unifying philosophy: likely a new skill (`/address-pr-feedback` or similar) invokable manually OR via a Linear ticket of that type — sibling-tool route only earns its place if the trigger must live outside Linear.

The **genuinely new design surface** is the per-ticket **required-skill-sequence contract**: which skills must run, in what order, with what severity-gates, before Done. Plausibly Linear-label-driven (mirroring `repo:` / `model:` from decisions 5 and 7) — e.g. `flow:plan-implement-verify` opts the ticket into the full lifecycle; default for ops-slot issues stays minimal.

**Why:** the failure mode being solved is single complex tickets where the implementing agent forgets details or under-verifies. The current drain shape trusts one session's self-assertion and has no answer to "ticket is fine but agent missed half of it."

**How to apply:** when proposing new drain-cycle features, prefer ones that strengthen the collaboration/verification axis (planner pass, verifier pass, PR-feedback loop) over throughput (parallelism, cycle chaining). When proposing automation, surface the trigger model — the operator dislikes always-on daemons (see decision 8: manual cycle runs are deliberate). Validate each piece on a real ticket/PR before writing code; cheap prompt-only experiments first.

skills.name: crit:crit
skills.content:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/anton/.claude/plugins/cache/crit/crit/1.7.1/skills/crit

# Review with Crit

Review and revise code changes, plans, live pages (running dev servers, staging URLs), or local HTML files using `crit` for inline comment review.

## Step 1: Pass arguments to `crit`

The CLI auto-detects the review mode from its arguments. **Do not ask the user which mode to use.** Pass `docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md` through:

```
crit docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md               # file, dir, URL, .html — CLI auto-detects mode
crit --pr <num|url>            # GitHub PR (range mode)
crit --range <base>..<head>    # commit range (range mode)
crit                           # no args → branch diff
```
If no arguments, check conversation context:

1. A plan file was written earlier in this conversation → `crit <plan-file>`
2. Otherwise → bare `crit` (branch diff)

## Step 2: Launch crit and block until review completes

**CRITICAL — you MUST run this step. Do NOT skip it. Do NOT proceed without it.**

Run `crit` **in the background** using `run_in_background: true`:

```bash
crit <plan-file>   # specific file
crit               # git mode
```

If a crit server is already running from earlier in this conversation, `crit` automatically connects to it. Starting from scratch, it spawns the daemon, opens the browser, and blocks until the user clicks "Finish Review".

`crit` prints the review URL on startup (e.g. `Started crit daemon at http://localhost:<port>`). Relay it verbatim:

> **"Crit is open at http://localhost:<port>. Leave inline comments, then click Finish Review."**

**Do NOT proceed until `crit` completes.** Do NOT ask the user to type anything. Do NOT read the review file early. Wait for the background task to finish — that is how you know the human is done reviewing.

## Step 3: Read the review output

When `crit` completes, its stdout includes the path to the review file (e.g. "Review comments are in /path/to/review.json"). Read it.

The file contains structured JSON. Three comment types:
- `review_comments` (top-level, `r_`-prefixed IDs) — general feedback
- File comments (per-file `comments` array, no `start_line`/`end_line`) — about the file as a whole
- Line comments (per-file `comments` array, with `start_line`/`end_line`) — about specific lines

Identify all comments where `resolved` is `false` or missing. Unresolved comments may have `replies` — read them before acting.

<important if="a comment has a quote, anchor, or drifted field">
- `quote`: the specific text the reviewer selected — focus your changes on the quoted text rather than the entire line range
- `anchor`: use it to locate the current position of the content; line numbers may be stale after edits
- `drifted: true`: original content was removed or heavily rewritten — line numbers are approximate at best
</important>

## Step 4: Address each review comment

For each unresolved comment:

1. Understand what the comment asks for
2. If it contains a suggestion block, apply that specific change
3. Revise the referenced file (plan or code file from the diff) using Edit
4. Reply with what you did: `crit comment --reply-to <id> --author 'Claude Code' '<what you did>'` (reply bodies support markdown)
5. **Do not pass `--resolve`.** Resolving is the reviewer's call. Only add `--resolve` if the user explicitly asks.

Editing the plan file triggers Crit's live reload — the user sees changes in the browser immediately.

<important if="you are replying to multiple comments at once">
Use `--json` for a single bulk call instead of one invocation per comment:

```bash
echo '[
  {"reply_to": "c_a1b2c3", "body": "Fixed"},
  {"reply_to": "c_d4e5f6", "body": "Refactored as suggested"}
]' | crit comment --json --author 'Claude Code'
```
</important>

**If there are zero review comments**: inform the user no changes were requested and stop the background `crit` process.

## Step 5: Signal completion and start next round

**CRITICAL — you MUST run this step. Do NOT skip it. Do NOT proceed without it.**

Run the **exact same `crit` command from Step 2** in the background. The daemon is keyed by arguments — mismatched args spawn a new daemon instead of reconnecting. If Step 2 was `crit plan.md`, this must also be `crit plan.md` (not bare `crit`).

On subsequent calls, `crit` automatically signals round-complete first, then blocks until the next "Finish Review" click.

Tell the user: **"Changes applied. Review the diff in your browser and click Finish Review when ready."**

**Do NOT proceed until `crit` completes.** When it does, return to Step 3. If the user finishes with zero comments, the review is approved — stop the loop and proceed.

<important if="the user asks for a URL, a shareable link, or a QR code for the review">

```bash
crit share <file>
```

**Always relay the full output to the user** — copy the URL (and QR code if `--qr` was used) directly into your response. Don't make them dig through tool output.

To remove a shared review:

```bash
crit unpublish
```
</important>

<important if="you are about to add --qr to a share command">
Only use `--qr` in real terminal environments with monospace rendering. Skip it in mobile apps (Claude Code mobile) or web chat UIs — Unicode block characters won't render.

```bash
crit share --qr <file>
```
</important>

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.
   - Alert / SLO state — for "what's bro… [truncated]

{
  "priority": "IMPORTANT",
  "message": "agent-skills loaded. Use the skill discovery flowchart to find the right skill for your task.\n\n---
name: using-agent-skills
description: Discovers and invokes agent skills. Use when starting a session or when you need to discover which skill applies to the current task. This is the meta-skill that governs how all other skills are discovered and invoked.
---

# Using Agent Skills

## Overview

Agent Skills is a collection of engineering workflow skills organized by development phase. Each skill encodes a specific process that senior engineers follow. This meta-skill helps you discover and apply the right skill for your current task.

## Skill Discovery

When a task arrives, identify the development phase and apply the corresponding skill:

```
Task arrives
    │
    ├── Vague idea/need refinement? ──→ idea-refine
    ├── New project/feature/change? ──→ spec-driven-development
    ├── Have a spec, need tasks? ──────→ planning-and-task-breakdown
    ├── Implementing code? ────────────→ incremental-implementation
    │   ├── UI work? ─────────────────→ frontend-ui-engineering
    │   ├── API work? ────────────────→ api-and-interface-design
    │   ├── Need better context? ─────→ context-engineering
    │   └── Need doc-verified code? ───→ source-driven-development
    ├── Writing/running tests? ────────→ test-driven-development
    │   └── Browser-based? ───────────→ browser-testing-with-devtools
    ├── Something broke? ──────────────→ debugging-and-error-recovery
    ├── Reviewing code? ───────────────→ code-review-and-quality
    │   ├── Security concerns? ───────→ security-and-hardening
    │   └── Performance concerns? ────→ performance-optimization
    ├── Committing/branching? ─────────→ git-workflow-and-versioning
    ├── CI/CD pipeline work? ──────────→ ci-cd-and-automation
    ├── Writing docs/ADRs? ───────────→ documentation-and-adrs
    └── Deploying/launching? ─────────→ shipping-and-launch
```

## Core Operating Behaviors

These behaviors apply at all times, across all skills. They are non-negotiable.

### 1. Surface Assumptions

Before implementing anything non-trivial, explicitly state your assumptions:

```
ASSUMPTIONS I'M MAKING:
1. [assumption about requirements]
2. [assumption about architecture]
3. [assumption about scope]
→ Correct me now or I'll proceed with these.
```

Don't silently fill in ambiguous requirements. The most common failure mode is making wrong assumptions and running with them unchecked. Surface uncertainty early — it's cheaper than rework.

### 2. Manage Confusion Actively

When you encounter inconsistencies, conflicting requirements, or unclear specifications:

1. **STOP.** Do not proceed with a guess.
2. Name the specific confusion.
3. Present the tradeoff or ask the clarifying question.
4. Wait for resolution before continuing.

**Bad:** Silently picking one interpretation and hoping it's right.
**Good:** "I see X in the spec but Y in the existing code. Which takes precedence?"

### 3. Push Back When Warranted

You are not a yes-machine. When an approach has clear problems:

- Point out the issue directly
- Explain the concrete downside (quantify when possible — "this adds ~200ms latency" not "this might be slower")
- Propose an alternative
- Accept the human's decision if they override with full information

Sycophancy is a failure mode. "Of course!" followed by implementing a bad idea helps no one. Honest technical disagreement is more valuable than false agreement.

### 4. Enforce Simplicity

Your natural tendency is to overcomplicate. Actively resist it.

Before finishing any implementation, ask:
- Can this be done in fewer lines?
- Are these abstractions earning their complexity?
- Would a staff engineer look at this and say "why didn't you just..."?

If you build 1000 lines and 100 would suffice, you have failed. Prefer the boring, obvious solution. Cleverness is expensive.

### 5. Maintain Scope Discipline

Touch only what you're asked to touch.

Do NOT:
- Remove comments you don't understand
- "Clean up" code orthogonal to the task
- Refactor adjacent systems as a side effect
- Delete code that seems unused without explicit approval
- Add features not in the spec because they "seem useful"

Your job is surgical precision, not unsolicited renovation.

### 6. Verify, Don't Assume

Every skill includes a verification step. A task is not complete until verification passes. "Seems right" is never sufficient — there must be evidence (passing tests, build output, runtime data).

## Failure Modes to Avoid

These are the subtle errors that look like productivity but create problems:

1. Making wrong assumptions without checking
2. Not managing your own confusion — plowing ahead when lost
3. Not surfacing inconsistencies you notice
4. Not presenting tradeoffs on non-obvious decisions
5. Being sycophantic ("Of course!") to approaches with clear problems
6. Overcomplicating code and APIs
7. Modifying code or comments orthogonal to the task
8. Removing things you don't fully understand
9. Building without a spec because "it's obvious"
10. Skipping verification because "it looks right"

## Skill Rules

1. **Check for an applicable skill before starting work.** Skills encode processes that prevent common mistakes.

2. **Skills are workflows, not suggestions.** Follow the steps in order. Don't skip verification steps.

3. **Multiple skills can apply.** A feature implementation might involve `idea-refine` → `spec-driven-development` → `planning-and-task-breakdown` → `incremental-implementation` → `test-driven-development` → `code-review-and-quality` → `shipping-and-launch` in sequence.

4. **When in doubt, start with a spec.** If the task is non-trivial and there's no spec, begin with `spec-driven-development`.

## Lifecycle Sequence

For a complete feature, the typical skill sequence is:

```
1. idea-refine                 → Refine vague ideas
2. spec-driven-development     → Define what we're building
3. planning-and-task-breakdown → Break into verifiable chunks
4. context-engineering         → Load the right context
5. source-driven-development   → Verify against official docs
6. incremental-implementation  → Build slice by slice
7. test-driven-development     → Prove each slice works
8. code-review-and-quality     → Review before merge
9. git-workflow-and-versioning → Clean commit history
10. documentation-and-adrs     → Document decisions
11. shipping-and-launch        → Deploy safely
```

Not every task needs every skill. A bug fix might only need: `debugging-and-error-recovery` → `test-driven-development` → `code-review-and-quality`.

## Quick Reference

| Phase | Skill | One-Line Summary |
|-------|-------|-----------------|
| Define | idea-refine | Refine ideas through structured divergent and convergent thinking |

{
  "priority": "IMPORTANT",
  "message": "agent-skills loaded. Use the skill discovery flowchart to find the right skill for your task.\n\n---
name: using-agent-skills
description: Discovers and invokes agent skills. Use when starting a session or when you need to discover which skill applies to the current task. This is the meta-skill that governs how all other skills are discovered and invoked.
---

# Using Agent Skills

## Overview

Agent Skills is a collection of engineering workflow skills organized by development phase. Each skill encodes a specific process that senior engineers follow. This meta-skill helps you discover and apply the right skill for your current task.

## Skill Discovery

When a task arrives, identify the development phase and apply the corresponding skill:

```
Task arrives
    │
    ├── Vague idea/need refinement? ──→ idea-refine
    ├── New project/feature/change? ──→ spec-driven-development
    ├── Have a spec, need tasks? ──────→ planning-and-task-breakdown
    ├── Implementing code? ────────────→ incremental-implementation
    │   ├── UI work? ─────────────────→ frontend-ui-engineering
    │   ├── API work? ────────────────→ api-and-interface-design
    │   ├── Need better context? ─────→ context-engineering
    │   └── Need doc-verified code? ───→ source-driven-development
    ├── Writing/running tests? ────────→ test-driven-development
    │   └── Browser-based? ───────────→ browser-testing-with-devtools
    ├── Something broke? ──────────────→ debugging-and-error-recovery
    ├── Reviewing code? ───────────────→ code-review-and-quality
    │   ├── Security concerns? ───────→ security-and-hardening
    │   └── Performance concerns? ────→ performance-optimization
    ├── Committing/branching? ─────────→ git-workflow-and-versioning
    ├── CI/CD pipeline work? ──────────→ ci-cd-and-automation
    ├── Writing docs/ADRs? ───────────→ documentation-and-adrs
    └── Deploying/launching? ─────────→ shipping-and-launch
```

## Core Operating Behaviors

These behaviors apply at all times, across all skills. They are non-negotiable.

### 1. Surface Assumptions

Before implementing anything non-trivial, explicitly state your assumptions:

```
ASSUMPTIONS I'M MAKING:
1. [assumption about requirements]
2. [assumption about architecture]
3. [assumption about scope]
→ Correct me now or I'll proceed with these.
```

Don't silently fill in ambiguous requirements. The most common failure mode is making wrong assumptions and running with them unchecked. Surface uncertainty early — it's cheaper than rework.

### 2. Manage Confusion Actively

When you encounter inconsistencies, conflicting requirements, or unclear specifications:

1. **STOP.** Do not proceed with a guess.
2. Name the specific confusion.
3. Present the tradeoff or ask the clarifying question.
4. Wait for resolution before continuing.

**Bad:** Silently picking one interpretation and hoping it's right.
**Good:** "I see X in the spec but Y in the existing code. Which takes precedence?"

### 3. Push Back When Warranted

You are not a yes-machine. When an approach has clear problems:

- Point out the issue directly
- Explain the concrete downside (quantify when possible — "this adds ~200ms latency" not "this might be slower")
- Propose an alternative
- Accept the human's decision if they override with full information

Sycophancy is a failure mode. "Of course!" followed by implementing a bad idea helps no one. Honest technical disagreement is more valuable than false agreement.

### 4. Enforce Simplicity

Your natural tendency is to overcomplicate. Actively resist it.

Before finishing any implementation, ask:
- Can this be done in fewer lines?
- Are these abstractions earning their complexity?
- Would a staff engineer look at this and say "why didn't you just..."?

If you build 1000 lines and 100 would suffice, you have failed. Prefer the boring, obvious solution. Cleverness is expensive.

### 5. Maintain Scope Discipline

Touch only what you're asked to touch.

Do NOT:
- Remove comments you don't understand
- "Clean up" code orthogonal to the task
- Refactor adjacent systems as a side effect
- Delete code that seems unused without explicit approval
- Add features not in the spec because they "seem useful"

Your job is surgical precision, not unsolicited renovation.

### 6. Verify, Don't Assume

Every skill includes a verification step. A task is not complete until verification passes. "Seems right" is never sufficient — there must be evidence (passing tests, build output, runtime data).

## Failure Modes to Avoid

These are the subtle errors that look like productivity but create problems:

1. Making wrong assumptions without checking
2. Not managing your own confusion — plowing ahead when lost
3. Not surfacing inconsistencies you notice
4. Not presenting tradeoffs on non-obvious decisions
5. Being sycophantic ("Of course!") to approaches with clear problems
6. Overcomplicating code and APIs
7. Modifying code or comments orthogonal to the task
8. Removing things you don't fully understand
9. Building without a spec because "it's obvious"
10. Skipping verification because "it looks right"

## Skill Rules

1. **Check for an applicable skill before starting work.** Skills encode processes that prevent common mistakes.

2. **Skills are workflows, not suggestions.** Follow the steps in order. Don't skip verification steps.

3. **Multiple skills can apply.** A feature implementation might involve `idea-refine` → `spec-driven-development` → `planning-and-task-breakdown` → `incremental-implementation` → `test-driven-development` → `code-review-and-quality` → `shipping-and-launch` in sequence.

4. **When in doubt, start with a spec.** If the task is non-trivial and there's no spec, begin with `spec-driven-development`.

## Lifecycle Sequence

For a complete feature, the typical skill sequence is:

```
1. idea-refine                 → Refine vague ideas
2. spec-driven-development     → Define what we're building
3. planning-and-task-breakdown → Break into verifiable chunks
4. context-engineering         → Load the right context
5. source-driven-development   → Verify against official docs
6. incremental-implementation  → Build slice by slice
7. test-driven-development     → Prove each slice works
8. code-review-and-quality     → Review before merge
9. git-workflow-and-versioning → Clean commit history
10. documentation-and-adrs     → Document decisions
11. shipping-and-launch        → Deploy safely
```

Not every task needs every skill. A bug fix might only need: `debugging-and-error-recovery` → `test-driven-development` → `code-review-and-quality`.

## Quick Reference

| Phase | Skill | One-Line Summary |
|-------|-------|-----------------|
| Define | idea-refine | Refine ideas through structured divergent and convergent thinking |
| Define | spec-driven-development | Requirements and acceptance criteria before code |
| Plan | planning-and-task-breakdown | Decompose into small, verifiable tasks |
| Build | incremental-implementation | Thin vertical slices, test each before expanding |
| Build | source-driven-development | Verify against official docs before implementing |
| Build | context-engineering | Right context at the right time |
| Build | frontend-ui-engineering | Production-quality UI with accessibility |
| Build | api-and-interface-design | Stable interfaces with clear contracts |
| Verify | test-driven-development | Failing test first, then make it pass |
| Verify | browser-testing-with-devtools | Chrome DevTools MCP for runtime verification |
| Verify | debugging-and-error-recovery | Reproduce → localize → fix → guard |
| Review | code-review-and-quality | Five-axis review with quality gates |
| Review | security-and-hardening | OWASP prevention, input validation, least privilege |
| Review | performance-optimization | Measure first, optimize only what matters |
| Ship | git-workflow-and-versioning | Atomic commits, clean history |
| Ship | ci-cd-and-automation | Automated quality gates on every change |
| Ship | documentation-and-adrs | Document the why, not just the what |
| Ship | shipping-and-launch | Pre-launch checklist, monitoring, rollback plan |"
}

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/anton/.claude/plugins/cache/crit/crit/1.7.1/skills/crit

# Review with Crit

Review and revise code changes, plans, live pages (running dev servers, staging URLs), or local HTML files using `crit` for inline comment review.

## Step 1: Pass arguments to `crit`

The CLI auto-detects the review mode from its arguments. **Do not ask the user which mode to use.** Pass `docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md` through:

```
crit docs/initiative-multi-agent-collaboration.md               # file, dir, URL, .html — CLI auto-detects mode
crit --pr <num|url>            # GitHub PR (range mode)
crit --range <base>..<head>    # commit range (range mode)
crit                           # no args → branch diff
```
If no arguments, check conversation context:

1. A plan file was written earlier in this conversation → `crit <plan-file>`
2. Otherwise → bare `crit` (branch diff)

## Step 2: Launch crit and block until review completes

**CRITICAL — you MUST run this step. Do NOT skip it. Do NOT proceed without it.**

Run `crit` **in the background** using `run_in_background: true`:

```bash
crit <plan-file>   # specific file
crit               # git mode
```

If a crit server is already running from earlier in this conversation, `crit` automatically connects to it. Starting from scratch, it spawns the daemon, opens the browser, and blocks until the user clicks "Finish Review".

`crit` prints the review URL on startup (e.g. `Started crit daemon at http://localhost:<port>`). Relay it verbatim:

> **"Crit is open at http://localhost:<port>. Leave inline comments, then click Finish Review."**

**Do NOT proceed until `crit` completes.** Do NOT ask the user to type anything. Do NOT read the review file early. Wait for the background task to finish — that is how you know the human is done reviewing.

## Step 3: Read the review output

When `crit` completes, its stdout includes the path to the review file (e.g. "Review comments are in /path/to/review.json"). Read it.

The file contains structured JSON. Three comment types:
- `review_comments` (top-level, `r_`-prefixed IDs) — general feedback
- File comments (per-file `comments` array, no `start_line`/`end_line`) — about the file as a whole
- Line comments (per-file `comments` array, with `start_line`/`end_line`) — about specific lines

Identify all comments where `resolved` is `false` or missing. Unresolved comments may have `replies` — read them before acting.

<important if="a comment has a quote, anchor, or drifted field">
- `quote`: the specific text the reviewer selected — focus your changes on the quoted text rather than the entire line range
- `anchor`: use it to locate the current position of the content; line numbers may be stale after edits
- `drifted: true`: original content was removed or heavily rewritten — line numbers are approximate at best
</important>

## Step 4: Address each review comment

For each unresolved comment:

1. Understand what the comment asks for
2. If it contains a suggestion block, apply that specific change
3. Revise the referenced file (plan or code file from the diff) using Edit
4. Reply with what you did: `crit comment --reply-to <id> --author 'Claude Code' '<what you did>'` (reply bodies support markdown)
5. **Do not pass `--resolve`.** Resolving is the reviewer's call. Only add `--resolve` if the user explicitly asks.

Editing the plan file triggers Crit's live reload — the user sees changes in the browser immediately.

<important if="you are replying to multiple comments at once">
Use `--json` for a single bulk call instead of one invocation per comment:

```bash
echo '[
  {"reply_to": "c_a1b2c3", "body": "Fixed"},
  {"reply_to": "c_d4e5f6", "body": "Refactored as suggested"}
]' | crit comment --json --author 'Claude Code'
```
</important>

**If there are zero review comments**: inform the user no changes were requested and stop the background `crit` process.

## Step 5: Signal completion and start next round

**CRITICAL — you MUST run this step. Do NOT skip it. Do NOT proceed without it.**

Run the **exact same `crit` command from Step 2** in the background. The daemon is keyed by arguments — mismatched args spawn a new daemon instead of reconnecting. If Step 2 was `crit plan.md`, this must also be `crit plan.md` (not bare `crit`).

On subsequent calls, `crit` automatically signals round-complete first, then blocks until the next "Finish Review" click.

Tell the user: **"Changes applied. Review the diff in your browser and click Finish Review when ready."**

**Do NOT proceed until `crit` completes.** When it does, return to Step 3. If the user finishes with zero comments, the review is approved — stop the loop and proceed.

<important if="the user asks for a URL, a shareable link, or a QR code for the review">

```bash
crit share <file>
```

**Always relay the full output to the user** — copy the URL (and QR code if `--qr` was used) directly into your response. Don't make them dig through tool output.

To remove a shared review:

```bash
crit unpublish
```
</important>

<important if="you are about to add --qr to a share command">
Only use `--qr` in real terminal environments with monospace rendering. Skip it in mobile apps (Claude Code mobile) or web chat UIs — Unicode block characters won't render.

```bash
crit share --qr <file>
```
</important>

content.text:
One unresolved thread — c_315ba9. You replied: *"we don't need this, i've already planned many projects and issues so fully aware of the gaps - what should i answer now to shape this plan betteR?"*

So slice 1 (planner spike) drops. To replace what the spike would have told us, I need four answers from you so I can fold the gap-knowledge straight into §7 and §2.

questions.question: What's the planner's role inside the worker session?

options.label: Refine / enrich ACs
options.description: Planner reads the ticket, produces an expanded AC checklist + edge cases, hands it to the implementer in the same session. No re-slicing.

options.label: Decompose into sub-tasks
options.description: Planner breaks the ticket into ordered sub-tasks the implementer then ticks off; sub-tasks live in a worktree file, not new Linear issues.

options.label: Readiness gate
options.description: Planner checks ticket against an implementation-ready bar; if it fails, the worker halts with a `needs-refinement` comment on Linear instead of implementing.

options.label: Enrich + gate (both)
options.description: Planner does AC enrichment AND can halt on unfixable ambiguity. Combines the first and third options.

questions.question: Where does the planner output live?

options.label: Worker context only
options.description: Output stays in the worker's own session as a system message; nothing written to disk or Linear. Cheap, ephemeral, no audit trail.

options.label: Worktree file
options.description: Written to `plan.md` (or similar) in the worktree alongside the implementation. Survives in the PR; verifier can read it; grading template can quote it.

options.label: Linear comment
options.description: Posted as a comment on the ticket. Visible to operator, survives drain, but increases Linear noise per ticket.

options.label: Worktree file + Linear comment
options.description: Both — worktree file is the source of truth, Linear comment is a short summary pointer for operator visibility.

questions.question: Which skill plays the planner role?

options.label: /shape:planning-and-task-breakdown
options.description: Existing skill, already used manually for issue breakdown. Closest match but designed for human-driven sessions.

options.label: /shape:plan-review
options.description: Existing skill, evaluates an existing plan rather than producing one. Better fit if the ticket already carries plan-shaped ACs.

options.label: New drain-cycle-specific skill
options.description: Author a new skill purpose-built for the worker's pre-implementation step (smaller scope, no human prompts, machine-checkable output).

options.label: Bespoke prompt in worker
options.description: No skill — just a hardcoded prompt block in the worker's `claude -p` invocation. Cheapest; loses reusability.

questions.question: When does the planner run?

options.label: Every verify-labelled ticket
options.description: Planner always runs first on any ticket carrying the `verify` label. Simplest control flow; pays the token cost every time.

options.label: Only if ACs missing/thin
options.description: Worker inspects the ticket first; runs planner only when ACs are absent or below a threshold. More logic in the worker; cheaper on well-formed tickets.

options.label: Separate opt-in label
options.description: Planner is its own label (`plan`) independent of `verify`; operator chooses per ticket. Most explicit; more labelling overhead.

options.label: Drop planner from v1
options.description: Don't ship the planner in this initiative — only build the verifier. Re-evaluate planner once verifier data shows whether under-specified tickets are actually the failure mode.
